perturbation.original_text: The term 'Confidential Information' shall refer to any proprietary business, financial, and technical data disclosed by one party to the other. Each party shall determine what constitutes Confidential Information based on its internal policies.
perturbation.changed_text: The term 'Confidential Information' shall refer to sensitive business information disclosed by one party to the other. Each party shall determine what constitutes Confidential Information based on its internal policies. In case of dispute, the disclosing party's determination shall be deemed final and binding, irrespective of whether such information falls under trade secret protections or not.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the text to state the disclosing party's determination is final and binding even if the information doesn't qualify as a trade secret would likely be unenforceable and may be considered an unfair business practice violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts trade secret laws because it attempts to bypass the legal definition of a trade secret, potentially allowing a party to claim confidentiality over information that does not meet the criteria for trade secret protection, thereby hindering fair competition and violating provisions of the UTSA related to misappropriation.

perturbation.justification: The change introduces a legal contradiction because it allows one party to unilaterally define confidential information, potentially overriding legal standards for trade secret protection. While the provided law snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation clearly state that this change circumvents the legal definition of a trade secret and could violate the UTSA. The original text allows both parties to determine what constitutes Confidential Information based on internal policies. The changed text gives the disclosing party the final say, regardless of whether the information meets the legal requirements for trade secret protection. This directly contradicts trade secret laws, as it could allow the disclosing party to prevent fair competition by claiming confidentiality over information that doesn't legally qualify, as described in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall be protected for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall be protected for a period of one (1) year from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the protection period to one year may be insufficient for protecting legitimate trade secrets, potentially violating the reasonable protection required under trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secret laws, such as the UTSA, require reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. A one-year protection period may be deemed unreasonably short for certain types of confidential information, thereby potentially violating the requirement to take reasonable measures to protect the trade secret. The modification may allow misappropriation of confidential information due to an unreasonably short protection period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the protection period for confidential information from five years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state this may violate trade secret laws like the UTSA, which require reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, because a one-year period might be deemed unreasonably short. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "trade secret" and states that the owner must take "reasonable measures to keep such information secret." By reducing the protection period to one year, the changed text could be interpreted as failing to provide reasonable protection, leading to potential misappropriation as defined in the snippet.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of confidentiality shall survive termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of confidentiality shall not survive termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that confidentiality obligations do not survive termination could be problematic for protecting trade secrets or sensitive information beyond the agreement's term, especially if the information retains its value and confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the UTSA as it fails to provide continued protection for trade secrets beyond the termination of the agreement. Trade secrets can maintain their value and competitive advantage even after a contract ends, and therefore, the obligation to protect them should also continue. By ending the obligation, the modification facilitates potential misappropriation and undermines trade secret protection.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modification stating that confidentiality obligations do not survive termination of the agreement directly contradicts the principle of protecting trade secrets, potentially undermining their value even after the contract ends. The original text ensured continued protection, while the changed text eliminates it. The Law Explanation supports this by stating the obligation to protect trade secrets should continue even after a contract ends. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, allows for civil action if a trade secret is misappropriated, suggesting that trade secrets should continue to be protected. By terminating the confidentiality obligations, the modified text creates a legal loophole, making it easier to misappropriate trade secrets after the agreement's termination.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to its conflict of laws principles, and the prevailing party in any dispute arising hereunder shall not be entitled to attorney's fees.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the choice of law to California and disallowing attorney's fees could create a conflict. While choice of law is generally permissible, some California laws mandate attorney's fees for certain types of claims (e.g., wage disputes). The clause attempts to contract around statutory rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 218.5 allows for the recovery of attorney's fees in certain wage and hour disputes. The changed text's provision disallowing attorney's fees directly contradicts this statute by attempting to waive a right granted under California law.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 218.5. (a) In any action brought for the nonpayment of wages, fringe benefits, or health and welfare or pension fund contributions, the court shall award reasonable attorney’s fees and costs to the prevailing party if any party to the action requests attorney’s fees and costs upon the initiation of the action. However, if the prevailing party in the court action is not an employee, attorney’s fees and costs shall be awarded pursuant to this section only if the court finds that the employee brought the court action in bad faith. This section shall not apply to an action brought by the Labor Commissioner. This section shall not apply to a surety issuing a bond pursuant to Chapter 9 (commencing with Section 7000) of Division 3 of the Business and Professions Code or to an action to enforce a mechanics lien brought under Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 8400) of Title 2 of Part 6 of Division 4 of the Civil
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original contract by selecting California law and disallowing attorney's fees. California Labor Code Section 218.5, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates that courts "shall award reasonable attorney’s fees and costs to the prevailing party" in wage disputes. The changed text directly contradicts this mandatory provision by attempting to prevent the recovery of attorney's fees, thus creating a legal inconsistency as it attempts to waive a right granted under California law.

perturbation.original_text: No modification of this Agreement shall be valid unless in writing and signed by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: No modification of this Agreement shall be valid unless acknowledged verbally by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the requirement to verbal acknowledgment instead of a written agreement may contradict the Statute of Frauds in many jurisdictions, which requires certain contracts (like those involving significant sums or lasting longer than a year) to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds, codified as UCC § 2-201 in many states, requires that contracts for the sale of goods for $500 or more be in writing to be enforceable. Similarly, contracts that cannot be performed within one year also fall under the Statute of Frauds' writing requirement. The modified text, which requires only verbal acknowledgment, directly contradicts this requirement, rendering modifications to contracts covered by the Statute of Frauds unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-201. Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-201. Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds. (1) Except as otherwise provided in this section a contract for the sale of goods for the price of $500 or more is not enforceable by way of action or defense unless there is some writing sufficient to indicate that a contract for sale has been made between the parties and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought or by his authorized agent or broker. A writing is not insufficient because it omits or incorrectly states a term agreed upon but the contract is not enforceable under this paragraph beyond the quantity of goods shown in such writing. (2) Between merchants if within a reasonable time a writing in confirmation of the contract and sufficient against the sender is received and the party receiving it has reason to know its contents, it satisfies the requirements of subsection (1) against such party unless written notice of objection to its contents is given within 10 days after it is received. (3) A contract which does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (1) but which is valid in other respects is enforceable (a) if the goods are to be specially manufactured for the buyer and are not suitable for sale to others in the ordinary course of the seller's business and the seller, before notice of repudiation is received and under circumstances which reasonably indicate that the goods are for the buyer, has made either a substantial beginning of their manufacture or commitments for their procurement; or (b) if the party against whom enforcement is sought admits in his pleading, testimony or otherwise in court that a contract for sale was made, but the contract is not enforceable under this provision beyond the quantity of goods admitted; or (c) with respect to goods for which payment has been made and accepted or which have been received and

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the requirement for written modifications with verbal acknowledgment, directly contradicting the Statute of Frauds. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 2-201, explicitly states that contracts for the sale of goods for $500 or more are 'not enforceable...unless there is some writing'. The change from 'written' to 'verbal' removes this fundamental requirement, rendering modifications unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, creating a direct contradiction.
